findings.title: CORS allow_origins=['*'] with allow_credentials=True
findings.description: Wildcard CORS with credentials allows any origin to make authenticated requests
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker creates evil.com 2. User visits evil.com while logged into your app 3. evil.com JS reads session cookie and calls your API 4. Attacker steals user data
findings.impact: Full account takeover via cross-origin requests
findings.recommendation: Replace allow_origins=['*'] with explicit list of allowed origins

findings.title: AVT_AUTH_REQUIRED defaults to false - all endpoints unprotected
findings.description: auth_required=False by default means all job creation, file access, and API endpoints are open
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Deploy with default config 2. Anyone on the internet can create jobs, download results, access all data 3. No ownership checks enforced
findings.impact: Unauthorized access to all user data and system resources
findings.recommendation: Change default to auth_required=True, require explicit opt-out

findings.title: Production credentials in plaintext on disk
findings.description: test_login.py contains production email/password for aivideotrans.site. tmp_playwright/*.js files contain HTTP basic auth password. Both untracked but not gitignored.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Developer runs git add . 2. Credentials committed to git history permanently 3. Anyone with repo access has prod credentials
findings.recommendation: Delete test_login.py, rotate password for production, add to .gitignore

findings.title: PostgreSQL default password hardcoded in docker-compose.yml
findings.description: POSTGRES_PASSWORD: ${PG_PASSWORD:-avt_dev_2026} commits a guessable password with network_mode: host exposing port 5432
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker scans port 5432 2. Connects with avt/avt_dev_2026 3. Full DB access (users, sessions, jobs)
findings.impact: Full database compromise including password hashes and session tokens
findings.recommendation: Remove default, require PG_PASSWORD env var, add to .env only

findings.title: Both Dockerfiles run as root — no USER directive
findings.description: gateway and frontend-next containers run as root. Combined with network_mode: host, a container breakout gives full host access
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. RCE vulnerability in gateway or frontend 2. Attacker gets root shell in container 3. network_mode: host = full host network access
findings.recommendation: Add USER directives to both Dockerfiles

findings.title: No CI/CD pipeline — no automated security scanning
findings.description: No GitHub Actions, no GitLab CI, no CI of any kind. No automated dependency audit, no SAST, no secret scanning.
findings.exploit_scenario: Vulnerable dependency ships to production undetected
findings.recommendation: Add minimal CI with npm audit + pip-audit + secret scanning

findings.title: frontend-next/package-lock.json never committed
findings.description: Lockfile exists on disk but not in git. npm ci in Dockerfile resolves whatever registry serves at build time.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Compromised npm package publishes malicious patch 2. Next Docker build pulls it 3. Malicious code in production

findings.title: Password minimum length is only 6 characters
findings.description: Only validation: len(password) < 6. No complexity requirements, no breach checking, no rate limiting on login.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. User sets password '123456' 2. Attacker brute-forces common passwords 3. No rate limit blocks the attack
findings.recommendation: Require 8+ chars, add login rate limiting, check HaveIBeenPwned

findings.title: No .dockerignore — .env files baked into Docker images
findings.description: COPY . . without .dockerignore includes any .env, __pycache__, test files in the image layer
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Dev has local .env with API keys 2. docker build bakes it into image layer 3. Anyone with image access can extract secrets
findings.impact: Secret leakage via Docker image layers
findings.recommendation: Create .dockerignore files for gateway/ and frontend-next/

findings.title: All containers use network_mode: host
findings.description: Every service binds to host network. PostgreSQL 5432, gateway 8880, app 8876/8877 all directly reachable.
findings.exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker scans server ports 2. Directly connects to backend APIs bypassing gateway auth entirely
findings.impact: Auth bypass by connecting directly to upstream services
findings.recommendation: Use Docker bridge network, only expose Caddy port 443
